title: Do Real Research
developmental_frame: At 11–12, children can run a simple structured investigation of what real people think — a small survey or a set of interviews — count the responses, and spot patterns. This is a real step beyond Q2's 'check other people's claims': now they *generate* new knowledge by gathering their own data. Keep the sample small and real. It also lays the groundwork for the year-end launch, where knowing your customer beats guessing.
authentic_value_meaning: Value here is finding out what people actually think or want by asking properly, instead of assuming — real evidence you gathered yourself. It's the grown-up power of replacing 'I reckon' with 'I asked twelve people and here's what they said'.

motivation_lever.mechanism: The child becomes someone who can *find things out* — running a real mini-study and discovering something they (and often the adults) didn't know. The satisfaction of a surprising, data-backed finding, and being able to say 'I have evidence', is powerfully motivating and makes them feel genuinely capable.

artifact: A mini research finding: a clear question, data gathered from at least five real people, patterns counted, and a conclusion drawn — even if it surprises them.
success_criteria: The child wrote one clear research question they could actually answer by asking people.
They asked the same questions to at least five real people and recorded answers.
They counted the patterns and drew a conclusion supported by the data.

lessons.title: Ask one clear research question
lessons.concept: Research starts with one clear question you can answer by asking people — 'what snack do kids in my class most wish the canteen sold?' Vague questions ('what do people like?') give mush. A sharp question gives usable answers.
lessons.real_example: 'Would people my age pay for a homework-reminder app?' is answerable. 'Is technology good?' is not — too big, too fuzzy to gather real data on.
lessons.apply_task: Write one clear research question you genuinely want answered and could ask real people.
lessons.reflection_prompt: Could five people actually answer your question in a sentence? If not, sharpen it.
lessons.feedback_task: Run your question past a parent — is it clear and answerable, or too broad?
lessons.teach_back_task: Help a friend sharpen a fuzzy research question into an answerable one.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: AI can help you narrow a question, but the thing you're curious about is yours. And AI can't tell you what your real classmates think — only asking them can.
success_criteria: One clear, answerable research question is written.

lessons.title: Ask real people
lessons.concept: Ask the *same* questions to several people — that's what makes it research rather than a chat. Same questions, several people, answers written down. Consistency is what lets you compare and count later.
lessons.real_example: You ask five classmates the exact same three questions about canteen snacks. Because the questions were identical, you can line up the answers and see what most people said.
lessons.apply_task: Ask your question (plus one or two follow-ups) to at least five real people. Write down every answer.
lessons.reflection_prompt: Did asking everyone the same way feel a bit formal? That sameness is exactly what makes the results trustworthy.
lessons.feedback_task: After two people, check with a parent that your questions are getting useful answers before doing the rest.
lessons.teach_back_task: Explain to someone why asking everyone the *same* questions matters in research.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: AI can help you word neutral questions (ones that don't push people toward an answer). But the answers must come from real people — invented data is worthless and dishonest.
success_criteria: The same questions were asked to at least five real people and answers recorded.

lessons.title: Count the patterns
lessons.concept: Now turn answers into numbers. Tally how many people said each thing. Patterns you can count ('4 out of 5 wanted spicy snacks') are far stronger than a vague feeling ('people seemed to like spicy stuff').
lessons.real_example: Your tally: 4 of 5 want spicy snacks, 3 of 5 would pay RM2, nobody wanted sweets. Suddenly you can *see* what people want, in numbers.
lessons.apply_task: Tally your answers into counts. Note the clearest pattern in the numbers.
lessons.reflection_prompt: Did the numbers match what you expected before you started? Data often corrects our hunches.
lessons.feedback_task: Show your tally to a parent and check your counting is fair and accurate.
lessons.teach_back_task: Show someone how counting answers turns opinions into evidence.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: Count your own data by hand — it's a small amount and doing it yourself keeps you honest about what it really says.
success_criteria: Answers are tallied and the main pattern is identified.

lessons.title: Draw a conclusion
lessons.concept: Finish by stating what your data shows — your conclusion — in one clear sentence, backed by the numbers. Good researchers follow the data even when it disagrees with what they hoped.
lessons.real_example: 'Most kids my age want spicy snacks and would pay RM2 — so a spicy snack stall could work.' That conclusion is earned by real evidence, not a guess.
lessons.apply_task: Write your conclusion in one sentence, backed by your numbers. Note if it surprised you.
lessons.reflection_prompt: Did your evidence support your first hunch, or change your mind? Changing your mind because of data is a strength.
lessons.feedback_task: Present your question, data, and conclusion to your family and ask if the conclusion follows from the numbers.
lessons.teach_back_task: Walk someone through your whole mini-study, from question to evidence-backed conclusion.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: The conclusion must come from *your* data, honestly read — not from what you wish were true, and not from an AI guess. Let the evidence lead.
success_criteria: A one-sentence conclusion supported by the data is written.
The child can explain how the numbers back the conclusion.
